entry.title: Your Path to SAP Integration Suite Certification: Courses, Exam Prep, and Expert Tips
entry.content:
Certifications are a great way to upskill and validate your expertise. If you are a developer and work within the integration space, the key certification to consider is the SAP Certified Associate - Integration Developer. This guide will tell you more about this exam and what to do to prepare.  

 

About the Exam 

Information about the exams, including the number of questions, cut scores and sample questions are published on learning.sap.com/certifications. The recommended certification for developers working in the integration space is the SAP Certified Associate - Integration Developer.  

The certification website gives you a breakdown of topic areas covered in the exam and their weight, so you know what to focus on. Check the exam page for the most up to date information. 

The certifications are valid for 12 months. You can extend the certification by 12 more months by completing an assessment. You will receive a heads up from SAP to let you know when it’s time to renew your certification. 

 

How to Schedule the Exam 

You can schedule your SAP Integration Suite certification exam in two ways: 

If You Have a Learning Hub Subscription 

With an SAP Learning Hub subscription, you receive four exam attempts per year as part of your plan. You can use these attempts to take up to four different certification exams or to retake an exam if needed. 

To schedule your exam: 

  1. Log in to learning.sap.com. 
  2. Find the certification exam you want to take. 
  3. Click Schedule. This will take you to the Questionmark certification portal, where you can view and manage your exam dates. 
  4. On the Questionmark certification portal, click Exam dashboard from the top-level navigation. Then click Schedule an exam.  
  5. Find the certification exam you want to take from the list (there are a lot, and it’s easier to do a control / command + F). Click the calendar icon to select the desired date and time. Then click Save Appointment. 
  6. To view your scheduled exams, click Exam Appointments from the top-level navigation.  

If You Don’t Have a Learning Hub Subscription 

If you don’t have a Learning Hub subscription, you can purchase a set of certification attempts.  

You have three purchase options: 

  • One certification attempt, available to purchase here. 
  • Two certification attempts, including 10 hours of practice system access with it, available to purchase here. 
  • A bundle of six attempts (usable for different certifications or exam retakes if needed), available to purchase here. 

Once purchased, follow the scheduling instructions on the website to book your exam. 

 

How to Stay Certified 

The SAP Integration Suite certification is valid for one year. To maintain your certification, you’ll need to renew it annually. 

  • If you have a Learning Hub subscription: You can renew your certification by completing a short assessment covering the latest SAP Integration Suite innovations. 
  • If you don’t have a Learning Hub subscription: You must purchase a certification attempt and retake the full exam. You can do so here. 

For more details, visit the SAP certification renewal guide. 

 

What to Study for the SAP Integration Suite Certification: Learning Paths  

The best way to prepare for the exam is to take learning paths. Completing the learning paths along with their quizzes and hands on exercises will provide the knowledge and practice needed to pass the certification. Below are the recommended learning paths to pass the SAP Integration Suite certification exam: 

  • Managing Clean Core for SAP S/4HANA Cloud 
  • Developing with SAP Integration Suite learning path  

The learning paths take about 12 hours to complete, and they cover all relevant topics you need to pass the exam.  

 

Additional Resources to Support Your Learning 

While the learning paths cover all the important topics required to pass your certification exam, it’s always helpful to reinforce your learning with additional resources and hands-on practice. Here are some valuable materials to deepen your understanding of SAP Integration Suite and boost your confidence before the exam. 

Live Sessions – SAP Integration Suite 

Join expert-led live sessions on SAP Integration Suite to get hands-on guidance, ask questions, and stay updated on the latest developments. See the upcoming live sessions here. You need the Learning Hub subscription to access these sessions. 

SAP CodeJams 

Participate in free CodeJam sessions, where you can practice SAP Integration Suite in a collaborative, hands-on environment. These sessions are ideal for gaining real-world experience and troubleshooting with experts. More info here. 

 

SAP Integration Suite Missions & Tutorials 

Explore interactive missions and tutorials on the SAP Developers site. These step-by-step guides will help you apply what you’ve learned and build confidence before taking the certification exam. 

 

Your SAP Integration Suite Exam Prep Checklist 

Follow these steps to maximize your chances of passing the exam on your first attempt. 

  1. Understand the exam structure.  
    • Review the certification webpage to learn about the exam topics and their weight, so you know where to focus your studies. 
    • Familiarize yourself with the exam format, including the number of questions, time limit, and passing score. 
  2. Follow the recommended learning journey 
    • Complete all the recommended learning paths outlined in this post. 
    • Make sure to complete all hands-on activities and quizzes to reinforce your understanding. 
    • Alternatively, attend a classroom training to prepare for the certification. There, you will get access to a real system to complete exercises on your own. See the training page for upcoming classroom sessions. 
  3. Get more hands-on experience 
    • Practice using SAP Integration Suite in different scenarios. For example, you can complete missions in the discovery center.   
    • Experiment with SAP Integration Suite features beyond the course exercises to deepen your understanding. 
  4. Create a study plan 
    • Set a realistic study schedule. Spread your learning over a few weeks rather than cramming at the last minute. 
  5. Take a Practice Test  
    • Use the sample questions provided by SAP to get a feel for the exam format and question style. 
  6. Rest well before the exam 
    • Avoid last-minute cramming. It’s better to be well-rested and focused. 
  7. Read Questions Carefully During the Exam 
    • Pay attention to keywords like "best," "most effective," or "first step." 
    • Eliminate obviously wrong answers before choosing the best one. 
    • If unsure, mark the question for review and return to it later. 

 

Share Your Certification Journey and Help Others Succeed 

Earning your SAP Integration Suite certification is a great achievement, and your experience can help others on the same path. Share your journey in the Integration Group by posting about your study strategies, favorite resources, and exam tips. Whether it’s a learning method that worked well for you or a resource you found particularly useful, your insights could make a big difference for those preparing for the exam.

entry.title: Hurdles / Pitfalls in End-User Training for Software Implementation Programs
entry.content:
We all know that when an organization decides to modernize its legacy systems for new technology, End-User Training becomes critical for the successful transformation. However, several pitfalls / hurdles can undermine the effectiveness of End User training.

Based on my experience of leading End User Adoption activities for various system implementation programs (e.g., SAP, Oracle, Ariba, Microsoft Dynamics, custom applications & platforms, etc.), I will share the key hurdles to watch out for.

Training Content Not User-centric

We happen to draft End-User training content, which is too technical, too general, or unclear & irrelevant. For example, the End-User training decks just show a dump of some as-is workflow diagrams (prepared by technical team) along with random system screenshots. OR, the End-User training decks have many references to configurations, integrations, middleware, technical system rules, back-end automated activities, etc. Such training content clearly indicates that the content is not user-centric. Such training decks make the understanding of new system even harder for the End Users.

To avoid this, we must do careful & quality Instructional Design to generate result-oriented learning experiences for corporate users, for example:

  • Drive the learning experiences by clear objectives, drafted around the expected user behavior linked to the execution of actual business operations in the new system.
  • Focus on real-world scenarios & actual business processes, plus do the required detailing of Concepts, New Terminology, & Processes.
  • Do easier scaffolding of information to draft simple, visual, and well-structured materials tailored to the audience.
  • Drive the content coverage & presentation from the perspective of how adults learn and how their learning psychology works.
  • Identify & highlight the crucial areas for key learning takeaways throughout the learning experiences.
  • Achieve user-engagement through various creative ways, e.g., content gamification, hands-on experiences, real-life scenarios, etc. to keep the learners hooked to the content and push the required learning.

Remember that if the training sessions are boring or irrelevant, users may lose interest and not absorb the information.

Training Content Not Tailored to Roles

Learning Consultants tend to think of one-size-fits all training, and ignore different roles and skill levels while drafting the content. To avoid this, we can tailor training to account for different user groups or job roles and workflows across departments in the client’s organization. Generally, the End-User training for new system implementation needs to encompass various roles, such as Warehouse workers, Finance teams, Procurement staff, etc.

The role-based training will educate end-users only on the features that they need, for example, Accounts Payable (AP) Staff will learn invoice processing, and Finance Managers will learn Reporting tools.

If the same generic training is given to all users, employees will struggle to find relevant information in the training content, and may skip the training sessions or get disengaged. This will eventually increase errors after go-live, causing operational delays.

Poor Planning and Timing for End-User Training

Generally, it is observed that End-User training sessions are scheduled too early, which makes the end-users forget what they have learned before go-live. For example, SAP training was delivered 3-4 months before go-live. By the time employees need to use the system, they forget most of what they learned.

On the other hand, if we conduct training sessions too late, it leads to the rushed sessions before go-live, or we squeeze the training into a short timeframe. This way, we do not allow enough time for hands-on practice and feedback.

Poor planning and timing will lead to the increased support tickets overwhelming the IT Support Team and Functional SAP Consultants. This eventually will drop the productivity as employees will struggle to complete tasks.

As a solution, we can conduct the training closer to go-live, offer refresher sessions post implementation, and provision just-in-time performance support knowledge base.

Inadequate Training Methods

While executing the end-user training sessions, we over-rely on passive training methods, for example, PowerPoint-driven instructor sessions / lectures, instead of interactive learning, or modern learning approaches like micro-learning, gamification, self-paced modules, on-demand videos, etc.

For ideal results, we can creatively use a blended approach, for example, self-paced modules, live demos, hands-on practice, self-help performance support material, etc.

Also, if we do not provision a sandbox environment / interactive hands-on practice for users to practice safely, this leads to poor user-adoption. If the training is more theoretical using PowerPoint presentations, rather than live demonstrations or sandbox environment, End-Users will not get the confidence and will struggle with actual transactions during go-live.

Failure to Address Change Resistance

If the End-Users do not feel positive about the change and carry the change-resistance in their minds, they will not get engaged in the training experiences, and will not take the training seriously. Hence, the organization should not assume that employees would embrace the new system (e.g., SAP) because the leadership mandated it.

If there is no clear communication on why the change is happening or how it will benefit the users, they will not make serious efforts to acquire the learning. We need to remember that negative attitude from users will make the adoption even harder.

As a solution, we need to implement a change management strategy, with leadership involvement and user champions to drive the adoption. The change management efforts should go hand-in-hand with the End-User adoption efforts.

No Change Champions or Super Users

Organizations treat training as a one-time event rather than ongoing process, and they undermine the need of Change Champions & Super Users to support and reinforce the ongoing learning.

As Learning Consultant, we need to guide the client on the role of Change Champions & Super Users, and how to identify & qualify them. For example, Change Champions & Super Users should have strong understanding the system and processes; they should be involved in various implementation activities (e.g., design sessions, system demos, testing, etc.) right from the beginning.

No Post-training Support

Generally, I have observed that once the training is done, users have no-easy way to get help when they face problems. Also, the IT / Helpdesk teams are not prepared to answer the questions.

The post-training support must be in place to ensure the long-term success. If we do not offer follow-up sessions, refresher sessions, on-demand learning materials & support; FAQs, Quick Reference Guides, and ongoing learning opportunities, then it will weaken the transfer of learning to real-life. This will eventually overwhelm the Helpdesk with calls and tickets.

Here, as a solution, we can offer ongoing support, including quick reference guides, advanced performance support framework & knowledge base; on-demand assistance by Change Champions & Super Users, dedicated Support Members for tricky SAP modules (e.g., Finance transactions) for troubleshooting; dedicated Help Desk to assist with adoption issues, and so on.

Failure to Measure Training Effectiveness

For training effectiveness, we rely mostly on attendance and do not assess the real knowledge retention & transfer, and the cascading work productivity impacts. For example, we tend to ignore the follow-up surveys or feedback loops for improve training; tend to ignore the metrics to track the post-training performance to measure adoption, etc.

As a solution, we can evaluate the End-user Training effectiveness through various metrics, such as Participation Metrics, Performance Metrics, Business Impact Metrics, System Usage Metrics, etc. We can include one or more KPIs (Key Performance Indicators) under these metrics. Read this blog for more information. 

Conclusion

It is essential to avoid the above pitfalls / hurdles to generate the effective end-user training experiences.

entry.title: SAP Standard Content Vs. Custom-built Content for End-User Adoption in SAP Implementation Programs
entry.content:
I have been a part of various SAP Implementation programs to lead the efforts for End-User Adoption. During various customer engagements, I have observed the SAP Standard Content, plus have anchored teams to build plentiful Custom Content for End User trainings.

In this blog, I will share my findings on whether we can use SAP Standard Content alone for End-User training / adoption.

SAP provides the standard content through various ways, such as SAP Learning Hub, SAP Enable Now, and Help Documentation. Remember that SAP standard content provides good information; however, using the standard content alone for End-User Training is generally not sufficient. 

Standard Content is generic in nature

Standard content is typically the generic content provided by SAP. This standard content is designed for a broad industry; it intends to be flexible to cover a wide range of industries and business functions.

The standard SAP content is drafted with model company, model configurations, best practices, and out-of-the-box functionalities. Some standard content does happen to be the cross-industry content, for example, the standard content like Finance, Procurement, HR, and Supply Chain applies across industries.

While this standard content from SAP is definitely good, but it is not tailored to specific industry or organization. This standard content does not fully address the organization-specific processes, configurations, custom workflows, business rules, policies, local regulations, legacy system integrations, etc. made during the implementation for the organization.

Standard Content lacks the implementation story / context

End Users typically prefer practical & role-based training, with real organizational processes. Additionally, while learning about process executions in the new system, the End Users also wish to get the following  insights integrated with the process explanations, precisely & realistically:

  • SAP integrations with their organization's legacy systems
  • Business rules & SOPs
  • What’s changing information
  • Cross-role collaboration
  • Inter-departmental connections
  • Organization-specific settings & controls configured in the system
  • Localizations
  • ...and more

Standard SAP documentation assumes SAP-centric environment, and generally does not cover the above-mentioned areas for the organization. In short, the standard content does not align directly with organization-specific user roles, integrations, policies, plus the overall implementation story & context; thus, making it difficult for End Users to apply the standard content in daily tasks. As a result, this leads us to the development of custom training content for End Users.

Standard Content does not reflect organization-specific workflows

In the initial phase of the implementation program, Functional / Technical Consultants gather requirements for the implementation, do Fit-Gap analysis, and then define multiple TO-BE business workflows, specific to the client's organization. These TO-BE workflows (typically swim-lane diagrams) cover how the organization's real business processes will be facilitated through the new system, encompassing various business roles, business rules, policies, legacy systems, and inter-departmental connections. Every such workflow tells a story from the organization’s perspective.

Now, here remember that these TO-BE workflows will play a crucial role for the following two scenarios:

  • First, Application Development Team / System Configuration Team need to refer to these workflows to develop & configure the new system to make it behave, exactly as expected by the client.
  • Second, Business Users / End Users must comprehend these organization-specific TO-BE workflows in a simplified way to know the new system's behavior and their interactions with the new system.

Here, the standard content from SAP will help the users understand the processes in general, but not in the context of the client's organization. As a result, this leads us to the development of custom content, explaining these TO-BE workflows in a simplified way (e.g., happy flows, alternate flows, exceptions, and other variables).

Standard Content does not reflect organization-specific system settings & controls

Here, let's assume that the SAP system is implemented with out-of-the box functionalities (with no customizations) for a client's organization. Now, even with out-of-the-box functionalities, the Implementation Team need to modify / customize certain aspects during the system set up / configuration, for example:

  • Configuring organization-specific product / material codes, catalogs, pricing conditions, roles-security constraints, order types, localization settings, and so on.
  • Enabling and disabling various controls in the system to make the system behave as per organization's requirements.

Now, when it comes to training, End Users prefer to learn the new system along with all these specifics configured / set in the new system. The standard content will not reflect the client-specific settings & controls, as it is drafted based on model company, configurations, settings, and SAP-centric environment. As a result, this leads us to the development of custom content to make it highly specific to the End Users.

Documentation Vs. Learning Experience

SAP’s standard content can be considered more like good product documentation / manual, explaining SAP’s product features, functional capabilities, and process overviews with probable business scenarios. This standard content is good reading material as a great starting point. However, we cannot achieve the required training / learning experience with the standard content.

Learning experience is typically driven by clear learning objectives tailored to user needs; practical & organization-specific context for better relevance; interactive elements (quizzes, simulations, scenario-based learning, micro-learning, real-life examples, interactive exercises, quiz, etc.) for better user engagement and retention, and so on.

Hence, if the goal is skill-building, knowledge retention, and behavioral change in End Users, then the carefully custom-built & instructionally-sound training is the best approach.

Consistency in Future Onboarding

When an organization undertakes a large-scale implementation and invests in Training, the organization also thinks about the future returns on the investment. For example, the organization wants to utilize the training materials for future onboardings of new hires, or use the training materials when employees leave or switch roles, etc.

  • Having custom-built training content with the organization-specific context & details ensures that every new hire receives the same level of knowledge & skills. If the organization simply relies on the standard content, then it will result in longer & inconsistent onboarding.
  • Having well-documented custom training materials help to maintain knowledge continuity as employees leave or change roles.

Different User Roles Using SAP in Unique Ways

In an organization, there will be different roles, who will interact with the new SAP system in unique ways, and will also have different comfort levels to use the modern technology / systems. For example, there will be roles, such as Warehouse Workers, Finance Teams, HR Staff, Procurement Specialists, etc. 

Now, when the transformation is planned to happen in a big way (in just a few months) from old to modern systems, these roles will have their own apprehensions & reservations about how they will transition to the new world suddenly.

Here, to safely & carefully take these users to the new world and maintain the momentum for business operations in the new system from day 1 after the system go-live, custom training content needs to be built. This custom content will precisely & realistically reflect the daily workflows plus scenarios and system navigation for each role. 

Some user groups may learn to use the new system just based on the standard content; however, we cannot generalize this for all user groups, specifically when the transformation happens at a large scale, and the organization's business operations & productivity will be at stake from day 1 after the go-live. In short, we cannot simply rely on the standard SAP content & generic system navigation, and expect that all user roles will transition confidently to the new world. 

Conclusion

Training Team can effectively use SAP’s standard content, user guides, and process documentation as a base to build the initial understanding quickly, and then proceed to fast-track the development of custom training content, in line with client-specific workflows, business rules, policies, legacy integrations, what’s changing information, system settings, cross-role collaboration, and so on.

On the other side, as part of the flipped learning approach, organizations can use the SAP’s standard content as a great starting point to familiarize their employees with the new SAP product / system, its features, process overviews, functional capabilities, etc. and then formally execute the custom-built training content sessions. This ensures that end-users understand and effectively use the new system in their daily work.

entry.title: How to Measure / Evaluate End User Adoption Training for System Implementation Programs
entry.content:
I have been acting as a Learning Consultant for various End-User Adoption programs for varied software implementations like SAP, Ariba, Oracle, Maximo, Microsoft Dynamics, and other custom technologies & platforms, for the clients across geographies.

Training Evaluation / Measurement is a critical area for the success of End-User Training. Typically, for training evaluation, we leverage the famous Kirkpatrick model with various levels of evaluation.

Based on my experience & research, I would like to share that we can also simply consider a set of KPIs (Key Performance Indicators) to carry out training evaluation. Here, I will explain the Training Evaluation, mainly for corporate end-user training programs in the context of technology implementation / transformation, for example, End-User Training for SAP implementation.

Based on my experience & research, I have tried to simplify & summarize the metrics as follows. You may want to consider these for training evaluation:

  • Participation Metrics
  • Performance Metrics
  • Impact Metrics
  • Usage Metrics

Let’s get familiar with these metrics, one by one.

Participation Metrics

For Participation Metrics, you may consider the following:

  • Training Completion Rate: Here, we consider the percentage of employees who complete the assigned training.
  • Attendance Rate: Here, we consider the percentage of employees who attend live or virtual training sessions, or complete the self-paced learning modules.
  • Drop-off Rate: Here, we consider the number of employees who start but do not complete the training.
  • Time Spent on Training: Here, we consider the average time employees spend on training modules.

Performance Metrics

For Performance Metrics, you may consider the following:

  • Assessment Scores: Here, we consider the average pre- and post-training test scores to measure knowledge improvement.
  • Certification Rate: Here, we consider the percentage of employees who earn certifications upon training completion.
  • Knowledge Retention Rate: Here, we use the follow-up assessments (e.g., 30/60/90 days post-training) to measure the retained knowledge.

Impact Metrics

For Impact Metrics, you may consider the following:

  • Employee Feedback Scores: Here, we consider the satisfaction ratings based on the post-training surveys.
  • Trainer Effectiveness Score: Here, we consider the feedback on trainer performance through evaluations.
  • Application of Learning: Here, we consider the percentage of employees who report applying new skills in their roles.
  • Reduction in Support Requests: Here, we consider the decrease in IT or Support tickets related to training topics. A high volume of support requests relating to SAP usage suggests that users struggle despite training.
  • Productivity Improvement: Here, we consider the changes in employee performance before and after training.
  • Production Milestone Achieved: Here, we consider the number of units / products completed during a specific period after go-live, for example, number of orders processed in the first week.
  • Error Rate Reduction: Here, we find out whether the training led to fewer mistakes or compliance violations over a period of time.
  • Time to Proficiency: Here, we evaluate the speed at which employees reach full competency in their roles.
  • Reduction in Process Cycle Time: Here, we consider the time taken to complete key processes, for example, order fulfilment, approvals, etc. 
  • Self-Sufficiency Rate: Here, we track how many users can complete tasks without additional support or helpdesk assistance.
  • Customer Satisfaction Improvement: Here, we look at the increase in customer satisfaction scores post implementation as users start using the new system post-trainings.
  • ROI of Training: Here, we compare training costs against measurable business improvements (e.g., increased efficiency, reduced errors).

Usage Metrics

Usage Metrics help determine whether users are actively using the new system (e.g., SAP) after training. For Usage Metrics, you may consider the following:

  • Login Frequency: Here, we track how often users log in to SAP. A low frequency may indicate a lack of confidence or resistance to the system.
  • First-Time Login Rates: Here, we measure how many trained users log in after training. A low rate suggests they may need additional support.
  • Time to First Transaction: Here, we identify how long it takes for users to perform their first meaningful action in SAP. A long delay might indicate training gaps.
  • Transaction Success Rate: Here, we track the percentage of completed transactions without errors. A high failure rate suggests training gaps.
  • Average Time per Transaction: Here, we compare how long it takes users to complete transactions before and after training. If the time decreases, training was effective.
  • Time Saved per Task: Here, we look at the reduction in effort required for various tasks.
  • Tasks Completed per Employee: Here, we look at the increase in the number of tasks completed per user post-training. 
  • Error Rates per Transaction: Here, we identify how often users encounter system errors, such as data entry mistakes or workflow issues.
  • Rework or Corrections Required: Here, we measure how often users need to correct data entries, which indicates training effectiveness.

Note: If you get low results for Usage KPIs, there could be several other issues in addition to Training, for example, e.g., system downtime, system glitches, technical issues, etc.

Data Sources

To collect data for various metrics, we can consider various data sources, such as:

  • Surveys, Learning Management System, User Feedback Sessions
  • Digital Adoption Platform Reports to check the consumption of In-app learning
  • Departmental Reports, Business Intelligence Platform
  • Data from Help Desk Tools (e.g., ServiceNow)
  • Built-in System Logs & Reports
  • Built-in Software Analytics to check page views, user interactions, tasks per user, etc.
  • SSO & Authentication Logs to check login frequency and daily/weekly/monthly active users
  • Various custom APIs enabled in the system
  • ...and so on

An Example

Now, let’s consider one imaginary example of SAP Training Program to understand the application of these training evaluation metrics at a high level.

  • Imaginary Company Name: Global Manufacturing Corp (GMC)
  • Training Program Name: SAP S/4HANA Implementation for Finance & Procurement Teams
  • Scenario: Global Manufacturing Corp is transitioning from legacy ERP systems to SAP S/4HANA. To ensure a smooth transition, they conduct a corporate training program for 200 finance and procurement employees. The training includes:
    • E-learning modules (basic SAP navigation, finance, and procurement processes)
    • Live Workshops / Instructor Sessions with SAP consultants (Trainers)
    • Hands-on practice in a sandbox SAP environment

Now, let’s try applying the Participation, Performance, Impact, and Usage metrics for this scenario.

Participation Metrics

A few examples of Participation Metrics are mentioned below.

  • Training Completion Rate: 92% of employees completed the assigned SAP training modules.
  • Attendance Rate: 88% attend at least one live SAP session with Instructor.
  • Drop-off Rate: 8% of employees start but do not finish the self-paced training.

Performance Metrics

A few examples of Performance Metrics are mentioned below.

Assessment Scores:

  • Pre-training Test: Avg. score 50% (low familiarity with SAP)
  • Post-training Test: Avg. score 87% (significant improvement)
  • Certification Rate: 80% pass the final SAP proficiency test and receive internal certification.

Impact Metrics

A few examples of Impact Metrics are mentioned below.

  • Employee Feedback Scores: 4.3/5 based on a post-training survey.
  • Trainer Effectiveness Score: 4.6/5 based on feedback from workshop attendees.
  • Application of Learning: 85% of employees report being comfortable using SAP in daily tasks.
  • Reduction in Support Requests: IT helpdesk sees a 35% decrease in SAP-related tickets within 2 months.
  • Productivity Improvement: Finance teams report a 30% reduction in time spent on purchase order processing.
  • Error Rate Reduction: Invoice processing errors drop by 20%, reducing rework and delays.
  • Time to Proficiency: Employees reach full efficiency in 4 weeks instead of the expected 6 weeks.
  • ROI of Training: Organization recovers training costs in 5 months due to improved process efficiency and fewer errors.

Usage Metrics

A few examples of System Usage Metrics are mentioned below.

  • Login Frequency (Number of times users log in after training): Example - 80% of trained users logged in within the first week.
  • First-Time Login Rates (Percentage of users accessing SAP for the first time post-training): Example - 95% of users logged in within two days of training.
  • Time to First Transaction (How quickly users complete their first task): Example - 70% of users completed their first purchase requisition within 24 hours.
  • Transaction Success Rate (Percentage of transactions completed without errors): Example - 85% of purchase orders were created successfully on the first attempt.
  • Average Time per Transaction (Time taken to complete key SAP processes): Example - Time to create a purchase requisition reduced from 15 minutes (pre-training) to 8 minutes (post-training).
  • Error Rates per Transaction (How often users make mistakes): Example - Incorrect data entry errors decreased by 40% after training.
  • Rework or Corrections Required (Frequency of users having to redo transactions due to mistakes): Example - The number of rejected purchase orders decreased by 25%.
  • User Self-Sufficiency Rate (Percentage of users completing tasks without assistance): Example - 90% of users completed procurement transactions without supervisor help.

Conclusion

We can creatively use the above KPIs as necessary in the technology / system transformation programs to evaluate the success of End-User training programs.

entry.title: Your Path to SAP Build Certification: Courses, Exam Prep, and Expert Tips
entry.content:
Earning an SAP certification is a great way to upskill, validate your expertise, and advance your career. For those working with SAP Build, there are two key certifications to consider: 

  • SAP Certified Associate - Low-Code/No-Code Developer - SAP Build (focused on SAP Build Apps, Process Automation, and Work Zone for app development and automation). 
  • SAP Certified Associate - SAP Build Work Zone - Implementation and Administration (focused on configuring and managing SAP Build Work Zone for digital workplace experiences). 

If you’re looking to get certified, this guide will walk you through both options—covering required courses, exam details, study tips, and resources to help you prepare. 

 

About the Exam 

Information about the exams, including the number of questions, cut scores and sample questions are published on learning.sap.com/certifications. The two available certifications for SAP Build are: 

  1. SAP Certified Associate - Low-Code/No-Code Developer - SAP Build  
  1. SAP Certified Associate - SAP Build Work Zone - Implementation and Administration  

 

The certification website gives you a breakdown of topic areas covered in each exam and their weight, so you know what to focus on.  

 

 

The certifications are valid for 12 months. You can extend the certification by 12 more months by completing an assessment. You will receive a heads up from SAP to let you know when it’s time to renew your certification. 

 

How to Schedule the Exam 

You can schedule your SAP Build certification exam in two ways: 

If You Have a Learning Hub Subscription 

With an SAP Learning Hub subscription, you receive four exam attempts per year as part of your plan. You can use these attempts to take up to four different certification exams or to retake an exam if needed. 

To schedule your exam: 

  1. Log in to learning.sap.com. 
  2. Find the certification exam you want to take. 
  3. Click Schedule. This will take you to the Questionmark certification portal, where you can view and manage your exam dates. 
  4. On the Questionmark certification portal, click Exam dashboard from the top-level navigation. Then click Schedule an exam.  
  5. Find the certification exam you want to take from the list (there are a lot, and it’s easier to do a control / command + F). Click the calendar icon to select the desired date and time. Then click Save Appointment. 
  6. To view your scheduled exams, click Exam Appointments from the top-level navigation.  

 

If You Don’t Have a Learning Hub Subscription 

If you don’t have a Learning Hub subscription, you can purchase a set of certification attempts.  

You have three purchase options: 

  • One certification attempt, available to purchase here. 
  • Two certification attempts, including 10 hours of practice system access with it, available to purchase here. 
  • A bundle of six attempts (usable for different certifications or exam retakes if needed), available to purchase here. 

Once purchased, follow the scheduling instructions on the website to book your exam. 

 

How to Stay Certified 

SAP Build certifications are valid for one year. To maintain your certification, you’ll need to renew it annually. 

  • If you have a Learning Hub subscription: You can renew your certification by completing a short assessment covering the latest SAP Build innovations. 
  • If you don’t have a Learning Hub subscription: You must purchase a certification attempt and retake the full exam. You can do so here. 

For more details, visit the SAP certification renewal guide. 

 

What to Study for the SAP Build Certification: Learning Paths 

The best way to prepare for the exam is to take learning paths. Completing the learning paths along with their quizzes and hands on exercises will provide the knowledge and practice needed to pass the certification. 

Below are the most relevant learning paths for each certification. 

SAP Certified Associate - Low-Code/No-Code Developer - SAP Build Exam 

If you're new to SAP Build, start with this introductory learning path: 

  • Composing and automating with SAP Build the No-Code Way 

To fully prepare for the certification exam, you’ll also need to complete the following learning paths: 

  • Developing Apps with SAP Build Apps using Drag-and-Drop Simplicity  
  • Creating Processes and Automations with SAP Build Process Automation  
  • Designing SAP Build Work Zone  
  • Experiencing End-To-End SAP Build  

These courses cover all required exam topics and must be completed before taking the certification. 

Please note that in order to take the SAP Certified Associate - Low-Code/No-Code Developer - SAP Build exam, you must complete the hands on portion of the Experiencing End-To-End SAP Build learning path. The proctor during the exam will ask for proof of completion. It’s a good idea to have a screenshot of your work handy.   

 

SAP Certified Associate - SAP Build Work Zone - Implementation and Administration 

If you are new to SAP Build, begin with:  

  • Composing and automating with SAP Build the No-Code Way 

For in-depth preparation specific to SAP Build Work Zone, complete the learning path below. This learning path covers deployment, system administration, integration, and extensibility options for SAP Build Work Zone. 

  • Implementing and Administering SAP Build Work Zone 

 

Additional Resources to Support Your Learning 

While the learning paths cover all the important topics required to pass your certification exam, it’s always helpful to reinforce your learning with additional resources and hands-on practice. Here are some valuable materials to deepen your understanding of SAP Build and boost your confidence before the exam. 

2 Minutes of SAP Build 

This YouTube playlist, created by Daniel Wroblewski, offers quick, digestible videos covering key SAP Build concepts. It’s a great way to reinforce your learning in just a few minutes. 

Live Sessions – SAP Build 

Join expert-led live sessions on SAP Build to get hands-on guidance, ask questions, and stay updated on the latest developments. See the upcoming live sessions here. You need the Learning Hub subscription to access these sessions.  

SAP CodeJams 

Participate in free CodeJam sessions, where you can practice SAP Build in a collaborative, hands-on environment. These sessions are ideal for gaining more experience and troubleshooting with experts. More info here.

SAP Build Missions & Tutorials 

Explore interactive missions on the SAP Developers site. These step-by-step guides will help you apply what you’ve learned and build confidence before taking the certification exam. 

 

Your SAP Build Exam Prep Checklist 

Follow these steps to maximize your chances of passing the exam on your first attempt. 

  1. Understand the exam structure.  
    • Review the certification webpage to learn about the exam topics and their weight, so you know where to focus your studies. 
    • Familiarize yourself with the exam format, including the number of questions, time limit, and passing score. 

  2. Follow the recommended learning journey 
    • Complete all the recommended learning paths outlined in this post. 
    • Make sure to complete all hands-on activities and quizzes to reinforce your understanding. 

  3. Get more hands-on experience 
    • Practice using SAP Build in different scenarios. For example, challenge yourself to create a team onboarding app, or a budget approval process.  
    • Experiment with SAP Build features beyond the course exercises to deepen your understanding. 

  4. Create a study plan 
    • Set a realistic study schedule. Spread your learning over a few weeks rather than cramming at the last minute. 

  5. Take a Practice Test  
    • Use the sample questions provided by SAP to get a feel for the exam format and question style. 

  6. Rest well before the exam 
    • Avoid last-minute cramming. It’s better to be well-rested and focused. 

  7. Read Questions Carefully During the Exam 
    • Pay attention to keywords like "best," "most effective," or "first step." 
    • Eliminate obviously wrong answers before choosing the best one. 
    • If unsure, mark the question for review and return to it later. 

 

What’s Next After Getting Certified 

Earning your SAP Build certification is a significant accomplishment, and your experience can be invaluable to others preparing for the exam. Share your journey in the Application Development and Automation Group by posting about your study approach, the resources that helped you most, and any tips you found useful. Whether it’s a hands-on exercise that deepened your understanding or a study habit that kept you on track, your insights can support and inspire fellow learners on their path to certification.

entry.title: How to Learn SAP Build: A Beginner’s Guide
entry.content:
If you're new to SAP Build and wondering where to start, this guide will help you navigate the best resources to gain hands-on experience and build your skills. Whether you’re looking for structured learning, interactive challenges, or live expert guidance, this guide will set you on the right path. 

 

Learning Paths 

The best place to start is the learning paths. SAP offers structured learning paths that guide you step-by-step through SAP Build concepts. These courses include interactive lessons and exercises to help you master the platform. Most importantly, they include a link to a free learning environment where you can practice your skills hands on.  

Recommended Learning Paths: 

If you're new to SAP Build, start with this introductory learning path: 

  • Composing and automating with SAP Build the No-Code Way 

Then, explore these specialized learning paths to deepen your knowledge in specific areas: 

  • Developing Apps with SAP Build Apps using Drag-and-Drop Simplicity  
  • Creating Processes and Automations with SAP Build Process Automation  
  • Designing SAP Build Work Zone  
  • Implementing and Administering SAP Build Work Zone 
  • Creating Applications and Extensions using SAP Build Code 
  • Experiencing End-To-End SAP Build 

 

SAP Build Missions & Tutorials 

Take advantage of interactive missions on the SAP Developers site. These step-by-step guides will help you apply your knowledge and build confidence before taking a certification exam.  

Check out the latest SAP Build missions here. 

 

Live Sessions – SAP Build 

Join expert-led live sessions for beginner sessions and deeper dive content, ask questions and stay updated on the latest SAP Build developments.  

See the upcoming live sessions here. Please note, you need the Learning Hub subscription to access these sessions.

 

2 Minutes of SAP Build 

Looking for quick, digestible learning content? This YouTube playlist offers bite-sized videos covering key SAP Build concepts. It’s a great way to reinforce your knowledge in just a few minutes. 

Click here to watch the videos. 

 

SAP CodeJams 

Host or participate in SAP CodeJam sessions to practice SAP Build in a collaborative, hands-on environment. These events are perfect for gaining experience, troubleshooting challenges with experts, and networking with fellow learners. 

More info here. 

 

Hands-On Challenges 

Challenge yourself with hands-on challenges created by SAP Developer Advocates. These challenges help solidify your learning while making the process fun and engaging. 

See previous challenges here. 

 

Next Steps: Earn Your SAP Build Certification 

An SAP Build Certification is a great way to prove your knowledge and expertise in low-code/no-code development. Whether you’re looking to advance in your current role or break into a new one, certification can give you the competitive edge you need. 

Find out more about SAP Build certification here. 

 

Keep Building Your SAP Skills 

No matter where you are in your learning journey, SAP Build offers a variety of resources to help you succeed. Start with structured learning paths, engage with the community through interactive challenges, and take advantage of live sessions to deepen your expertise. Happy learning!

entry.title: Customer course UX100 (SAP Fiori – Foundation) available for SAP S/4HANA 2023
entry.content:
The course development of the UX100 is (again) finished. Let’s summarize all the new and updated topics in a central place. Welcome to my fourth blog post about the customer training UX100 (SAP Fiori – Foundation).

If you are more interested in a digital learning for SAP Fiori, please read my blog post:
SAP Learning Journey – Learning the Basics of SAP Fiori

Introduction

Starting in 2016, this is now the seventh incarnation of the introduction to SAP Fiori for on-premise. SAP Fiori moved on and therefore the UX100 moved on. With SAP S/4HANA 2023, the SAP Fiori groups are deprecated and were removed from UX100. In addition, the SAP Fiori launchpad designer is no longer recommended and was reduced to its core tasks, where no other tool is in place, yet. Finally, the appendix was dropped with all those long-ago-features from the SAP Business Suite.

The units in the current version are the following:

  1. End-User Perspective
  2. Technology
  3. Architecture
  4. Content Management
  5. Content Administration
  6. Adaptation
  7. Mobility
  8. Integration
  9. Further Information

If you are interested in an adapted version of the material, you can order a customer specific version of the course including only the topics you wish.

System Landscape

Let’s start with the system release: SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS02. Our system landscape – we call it universal target – is used in nearly all our technology courses and offers full access for the participants from the SAP Fiori launchpad (FLP) in the browser down to SAP HANA on the SUSE Linux Enterprise server (SLES). Everything is set up following the newest guidelines of SAP so that it can really act as a template for customers.

UX100 System Landscape (Screenshot from System Setup Guide)

In addition, we offer a self-service for participants to create a subaccount in SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) for the exercises. It is comparable to the SAP BTP trial but limited to services used in UX100 and only valid for the training week. If you need more time to test SAP BTP, please check out the SAP BTP trial.

User Experience

One goal of this update was to update all slides to the newest design guidelines of SAP. Nowhere can this be seen better as in the SAP Fiori launchpad itself. SAP Fiori with Horizon provides a fresh look and feel with new colors and a better user guidance.

How to Add Apps as Favorite to My Home Page (Screenshot from UX100)

The first thing users see when starting the SAP Fiori launchpad is the new My Home. It is a page just for the user, which can be personalized in many ways. Beside to dos, pages, and insights, the apps section can be populated with favorites. This can be done in two ways:

  1. Choose Add Apps in the Apps section to open the app finder and select an app from a catalog.
  2. On an SAP Fiori page assigned to your user in FLP, choose Edit Current Page in the User Actions Menu and choose Add to My Home in the context menu of a tile.

The first way comes in handy if you browse all apps assigned to you without concerning, if the app is already part of any page. It is a search for functionality rather than process. The second way instead allows to bring an app in the foreground, which may be the start of a bigger process the user regularly runs from a page. It is about getting faster access to this process.

For more information about the SAP Fiori user experience, please read the blog post of ThomasReiss:
User Experience Advances with SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS01 (Private Cloud and On-Premise)

Technology

The other ongoing goal is to keep track with all the advancements in SAP Fiori technologies. For example, TypeScript got introduced in SAPUI5 to ease the work of developers and consuming OData V4 services gets more and more common. The development tools continue to advance, too.

How to Access a Dev Space in Visual Studio Code (Screenshot from UX100)

The SAP Business Application Studio toolkit extension allows to connect and access dev spaces of SAP Business Application Studio (BAS) in Visual Studio (VS) Code. It can be found and installed in the extension marketplace.

The extension adds the SAP BAS Desktop Client as side panel. For the connection, the URL to access the SAP BAS is needed. The logon uses a Web browser window asking for your credentials and establishing an SSH connection in a new VS Code window. You can access and change the connection details choosing the blue SSH URL at the bottom of the window.

Accessing a dev space in VS Code loads all extensions of the dev space in the local VS Code window. This includes extensions, which are not available in the extension marketplace like Joule. In addition, you can add additional extensions from the extension marketplace.

You can start and stop dev spaces, open the dev space manager, and connect to multiple dev spaces of different accounts. All settings are saved in the dev spaces but are used locally in VS Code.

For more information about the SAP BAS in VS Code, please read the blog post of geert-janklaps:
Access SAP Business Application Studio as a remote from Visual Studio Code

Spaces and Pages

The heart of the UX100 will always be the content management of the SAP Fiori launchpad. One part of this is spaces and pages. Introduced in SAP S/4HANA 2020, they got some new features in the latest releases like sort priority or merging spaces and pages with other spaces and pages.

Merge of SAP Fiori Pages (Screenshot from UX100)

Sometimes users see the same apps several times or have similar pages assigned more than once, because they have similar roles assigned. For example, the sales manager and the sales accountant have overlapping tasks and therefore apps in their pages. Such pages can be merged to combine the apps or sections in one transient page at runtime. This means that the merged page is not visible in the Manage Launchpad Pages app, but just for the user in the SAP Fiori launchpad having all pages assigned.

Customer pages can be merged with other customer pages or with an SAP page. For the second one, you enter the name of the SAP page in the Merge ID field in the General Data tab of the customer page. When merging customer pages, you can define an own name for the merged page and enter it as Merge ID in all pages you want to merge.

For more information about merging spaces and pages, please read the blog post of KattiaJ-P:
Merging Spaces and Pages

Catalog Management

The other part of SAP Fiori content is catalogs. There were several advances in the latest releases, for example, the introduction of typed catalogs in SAP S/4HANA 2020. The tool for managing those catalogs is the SAP Fiori launchpad application manager (FLPAM). In SAP S/4HANA 2023, it got a new feature.

How to Adapt a Technical Catalog (Screenshot from UX100)

The adaptation mode in the FLPAM allows you to make (limited) adaptations in the launchpad app descriptor items (LADI) of SAP-delivered technical catalogs. The changes are directly available in all references in business catalogs.

Switching to the adaptation mode adds the Adaptation tab to the details of a LADI. Enlarge the tab and choose Edit: You can now select the Adapt checkbox for each property you want and enter your adaptation in the input field behind. Adapted values can be translated using the SE63.

For more information about adapting technical catalogs, please read the blog post of ruth_groene:
SAP Fiori Launchpad: New Fast and Easy Option to Adapt SAP-delivered Launchpad Content (for On-Premise and Cloud, Private Edition)

Key User Adaptation

There are so many other topics around SAP Fiori that there is just not enough room for everything in a 5-day-couse. But one topic was always there as an outlook: Key user adaptation. With key user adaptation or UI adaptation at runtime (RTA), SAP Fiori apps based on SAPUI5 that support RTA can be changed directly and intuitively in the SAP Fiori launchpad without developing any source code. With SAP S/4HANA 2023, versions of such app variants were introduced.

How to Activate and Publish a New Version (Screenshot from UX100)

After the adaptations to an app have been saved in a variant, they can be activated as a new version. This sets this variant as the active version for all users in the system replacing the original app or any previously active version. You can switch to an older version by activating it as a new version.

With the active version being selected, the Publish button can be used to assign it to a transport request. A customizing or workbench request can be selected to decide, if the adaptations should be available client-specific or cross-client in the follow-up system.

For more information about key user adaptation, please read the tutorial of Vitor Seifert:
Key User Adaptation for SAPUI5 Applications - UI Changes and Modes

Summary

If you have read so far, it seems that you are really thinking about attending the course. Don’t hesitate and check out the physical and virtual events we offer:

https://training.sap.com/course/UX100

If you are more interested in a digital learning, please visit https://learning.sap.com. Many parts of the UX100 are already available free-of-charge:

https://learning.sap.com/learning-journey/learn-the-basics-of-sap-fiori

For any other questions around the offerings of SAP Learning Services, please jump to our community page and get in contact with us:

https://pages.community.sap.com/topics/training-certification

I hope even if you won’t attend the UX100 in any kind, you found some useful information or references around SAP Fiori in this blog post.

Happy learning
Stefan

entry.title: Unlock Your Potential with the Updated C4H320 - SAP Commerce Cloud Business User Training
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

I'm glad to announce the release of our updated Instructor Lead Training course for SAP Commerce Cloud Business User Training.

C4H320 - SAP Commerce Cloud Business User Training

If you're looking to harness the full potential of SAP Commerce Cloud, the updated C4H320 - SAP Commerce Cloud Business User Training is an excellent opportunity for you. This instructor-led course is designed to empower business users with the skills and knowledge needed to effectively utilize SAP Commerce Cloud.

In this 3-day course, participants will learn about SAP Commerce features, concepts, and user interfaces from a functional perspective to get an all-round view of the software. This course contains demonstrations by trainers as well as hands-on exercises for course participants. The course presents a high-level overview of SAP Commerce Cloud and its important role in the SAP Customer Experience portfolio. Participants will have the opportunity to identify the main out-of-the-box features and perform exercises on how to provide basic configuration in Backoffice, SmartEdit and other administration tools.

Course Overview

The C4H320 training course is specifically tailored for business users who want to get the most out of SAP Commerce Cloud. Here’s a glimpse of what you’ll learn:

  • Understanding Core Features: Gain a deep insight into the core functionality of SAP Commerce Cloud. You will learn how to navigate the platform with ease and understand its primary components.
  • Enhancing User Experience: Discover how to enhance your customer's journey using SAP Commerce Cloud’s customizable storefront solutions and user-centric design features. Learn about strategies to improve user engagement and retention.
  • Omnichannel Integration: Learn how to create a seamless experience across multiple channels, ensuring your customers can engage with your business wherever they are, whether it’s mobile, online, or in-store.
  • Efficient Data Management: Understand how to manage data effectively within SAP Commerce Cloud for better business insights. Master data input, inventory management, and customer data handling to streamline operations.
  • Product and Content Administration: Equip yourself with knowledge about administering product catalogs and managing content efficiently, allowing you to update and adapt your offerings dynamically.

Benefits of Instructor-Led Training

Taking the C4H320 course as an instructor-led experience is a significant advantage:

  • Personalized Guidance: Direct interaction with experienced instructors provides personalized guidance and insights. Get answers to your questions in real-time and learn from practical examples tailored to your industry.
  • Hands-On Experience: Engaging in live demonstrations and interactive sessions ensures that you grasp concepts effectively and are well-equipped to implement them in real scenarios.
  • Networking Opportunities: Connect with other professionals with similar interests and share experiences. Collaborative learning creates a rich environment for exchanging ideas and solving common challenges.

Live System Access
The course is available for Live System Access, an easy way to perform the hands-on exercises of these courses via SAP systems. For more information, please refer to this blog post. If you want to enroll for this system you can go here.
